title: The Oscars Show Returns: The Five Best Movies at the Fall Festivals … So Far
format: Sean and Amanda open Oscar season from their living rooms, sorting through a shrunken, virtual Toronto slate for anything that can carry a best-picture campaign through an unusually long, uncertain race. Then Gerard Bush and Christopher Renz on Antebellum.

segments.heading: Notes From a Virtual TIFF
segments.note: what actually got watched this year

films.title: David Byrne's American Utopia
films.note: Spike Lee's film of Byrne's Broadway show. Both hosts call it the best thing they've seen this year; it lands on HBO October 18.

films.title: One Night in Miami...
films.note: Regina King's directorial debut, adapted from Kemp Powers's play. A fictionalized 1964 hotel-room conversation between Muhammad Ali, Malcolm X, Jim Brown, and Sam Cooke, shot by Tami Reiker. Amazon has it.

films.title: The Boy from Medellín
films.note: Matthew Heineman's documentary on J Balvin, made for Amazon. Sean, not a big fan of Balvin going in, was won over by its focus on an artist's anxiety and online criticism.

films.title: Pieces of a Woman
films.note: Vanessa Kirby and Shia LaBeouf, from Kornél Mundruczó and Kata Wéber. Opens with a roughly 25-minute unbroken take Amanda watched at 9 a.m. and found bracing. Netflix picked it up out of the festival.

films.title: City Hall
films.note: Frederick Wiseman's four-and-a-half-hour Boston documentary. Sean watched it in two two-hour sittings; Amanda admits she never got to it.

films.title: Ammonite
films.note: Saoirse Ronan and Kate Winslet for Francis Lee, from Neon. Both hosts had it circled as a lock and found it cold and static instead, missing the charge they wanted from the Portrait of a Lady on Fire comparisons.

films.title: Wolfwalkers
films.note: The Irish animated film everyone else seemed to respond to. Sean, self-appointed animation advocate on the show, missed it entirely.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: Chloé Zhao's adaptation of Jessica Bruder's book, with Frances McDormand as a fictional composite living among real-life nomads. Winner of the Golden Lion at Venice; the hosts saw it at a Telluride-sponsored drive-in by the Rose Bowl, ash from the California fires drifting onto the cars during a post-screening Q&A. Searchlight opens it December 4. Sean flags David Strathairn as a possible supporting nod, and can't picture how Zhao's next film, Marvel's Eternals, will square with what she does here.

segments.heading: The Best Actress Race Takes Shape

films.title: Pieces of a Woman
films.note: Vanessa Kirby, fresh off winning best actress at Venice, is the early frontrunner in Amanda and Sean's eyes, now with Netflix's campaign machine behind her.

films.title: Ammonite
films.note: Kate Winslet's reviews have been split, so the hosts aren't sure she's a lock this time despite her track record.

films.title: Nomadland
films.note: Frances McDormand chasing a third best actress statuette, a feat only Katharine Hepburn has managed. Sean also flags her next film, The Tragedy of Macbeth, opposite Denzel Washington and directed by her husband Joel Coen.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.guest: Gerard Bush and Christopher Renz on

films.title: Antebellum
films.note: The writer-directors' feature debut, starring Janelle Monáe as Veronica Henley. Bush says the premise came from a vivid nightmare he had after his father, best friend, and dog all died within a short span; the pair wrote it as a short story the same morning. Shot on a real plantation with recalibrated Gone With the Wind-era lenses; releasing direct to digital rather than delaying for theaters given the pandemic.

films.title: The Invisible Man
films.note: Bush's answer to the show's closing question: the last movie he saw in an actual theater before lockdown.

films.title: The Rental
films.note: Renz's pick for the last thing he watched and loved: Dave Franco's directorial debut, which he calls 'a super genre Airbnb nightmare.'

referenced.group: Last year's best-picture field, for comparison
